policy_statement: Implement measures to reduce food waste and follow up with annual measurement, with each operation reporting measures taken and evaluating effects (2024–2030).
mechanism_description: The policy implements measures to reduce food waste and uses annual measurement and reporting to track results, lowering the amount of organic waste that would generate methane in landfills.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste generation reduction → ↓ landfilled biodegradable waste → ↓ anaerobic decomposition → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: City operations and/or community supply chains generate less solid waste (often targeting food waste) through prevention, process changes, and purchasing/behavior shifts.  
2) Mediating process: Reduced mass of biodegradable waste entering landfills lowers anaerobic decomposition and methane formation (and can also reduce landfill-related transport/handling emissions).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions, dominated by avoided CH₄ from landfills over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided landfill methane emissions due to less waste (particularly organics) being disposed of in landfills.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Source reduction lowers the amount of waste ultimately landfilled, which reduces methane generation—this is a direct GHG mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although waste reduction can improve resource efficiency (less material throughput) and reduce upstream production emissions, the canonical mechanism specified is explicitly “landfill_methane_avoidance,” making the dominant intended and common climate effect mitigation via CH₄ reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a jurisdiction has no landfilling (e.g., most waste is incinerated with energy recovery) the dominant effect could shift toward avoided upstream production emissions or reduced combustion emissions; however, those cases would be a different mechanism definition than “landfill methane avoidance.”  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose mechanism is waste source reduction specifically to avoid landfill methane has mitigation as the primary category because the main climate lever is reduced CH₄ emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy is best tagged as municipal operations vs. community-wide, organics-specific vs. general waste prevention, and whether upstream embodied-emissions benefits dominate in a specific locale requires policy- and context-specific evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Where landfill gas capture is already near-complete, incremental methane avoidance from source reduction may be smaller; where landfilling is not the disposal route, the dominant benefit may shift away from landfill methane. Co-benefits (resource efficiency, cost savings) do not change the primary category for this mechanism.
key_indicators: "reduce food waste"; "follow up with annual measurement"; "Each operation reports"; "evaluates their effects"
co_benefits: Cost savings from reduced purchasing and disposal; improved operational efficiency; potential social benefits if surplus food is redirected (not specified).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a regulatory-mandate (internal operational requirement) or target-setting if a numeric reduction goal were stated; however, the explicit instrument in the text is the measurement/reporting follow-up.
